questions.question: Explain the concept of clipping in computer graphics. Why is it essential in the graphics pipeline?
keyPoints: Definition: process of eliminating portions outside viewing region
Viewports and windows are the clipping regions
Prevents processing of invisible primitives
Saves computational resources
Prevents memory access violations for out-of-bounds coordinates
Occurs after transformation, before rasterization

questions.question: Describe the Cohen-Sutherland line clipping algorithm with special mention of outcodes and three possible cases.
keyPoints: Outcodes are 4-bit codes representing endpoint positions
Bit order: top, bottom, right, left
Case 1: Both outcodes 0000 - completely inside, ACCEPT
Case 2: AND of outcodes ≠ 0 - completely outside, REJECT
Case 3: Otherwise - partially inside, calculate intersection and repeat
Efficient for trivial rejection/acceptance cases

questions.question: Explain the Liang-Barsky parametric line clipping algorithm. How is it more efficient than Cohen-Sutherland?
keyPoints: Uses parametric form: x=x1+u(x2-x1), y=y1+u(y2-y1)
Four parametric inequalities for boundaries
Calculates entering (u1) and exiting (u2) parameters
Compares u1 and u2 to determine visibility
More efficient because: direct computation without iterative subdivision
Works well for lines nearly parallel to boundaries

questions.question: Describe the Sutherland-Hodgman polygon clipping algorithm. How does it handle vertices at clipping boundaries?
keyPoints: Clips polygon against one boundary at a time sequentially
For each edge of polygon: test both endpoints
If both inside: add second vertex to output
If first inside, second outside: add intersection point only
If first outside, second inside: add intersection and second vertex
If both outside: add nothing
Creates new vertices at intersection points with boundary

questions.question: Differentiate between point clipping, line clipping, and polygon clipping with reference to their applications.
keyPoints: Point clipping: test if xwmin≤x≤xwmax and ywmin≤y≤ywmax - for small primitives
Line clipping: determine visible portions of lines - for wireframe rendering
Polygon clipping: clip complex shapes - for filled regions
Point is basic - just coordinate comparison
Line uses algorithms like Cohen-Sutherland, Liang-Barsky
Polygon uses Sutherland-Hodgman, Weiler-Atherton
Each handles increasing complexity of primitives

questions.question: What are the different methods of text clipping? Explain each briefly.
keyPoints: All-or-none string clipping: either show entire string or none
Individual character clipping: each character clipped separately
Stroke clipping: clip each stroke/segment of character
Pixel-level clipping: most precise, clips at individual pixel level
Selection depends on application requirements
Higher precision requires more computational cost

questions.question: Compare Cohen-Sutherland and Liang-Barsky line clipping algorithms.
keyPoints: Cohen-Sutherland: uses outcodes, iterative subdivision
Liang-Barsky: uses parametric equations, direct computation
Cohen-Sutherland: good for trivial cases, may need multiple iterations
Liang-Barsky: more efficient for complex cases, single computation
Cohen-Sutherland: easier to implement
Liang-Barsky: better for lines nearly parallel to boundaries
Both produce same results, different computational approach

questions.question: Explain the Cohen-Sutherland line clipping algorithm in detail with a suitable example. Include the concept of outcodes and trace the algorithm for a line from (10,10) to (90,90) with clipping window (20,20) to (80,80).
keyPoints: Define clipping window boundaries
Explain 4-bit outcode assignment
Bit representation: top=8, bottom=4, right=2, left=1
Three cases: trivial accept, trivial reject, clip
Step-by-step trace: (10,10) outcode=5 (left+bottom=1+4), (90,90) outcode=10 (right+top=2+8)
AND=0, so not trivial reject; neither is 0000, so not trivial accept
Calculate intersection with boundary
Continue until line is clipped
Final visible segment from (20,20) to (80,80)
Time complexity analysis

questions.question: Derive the parametric equations used in Liang-Barsky algorithm and explain how it clips a line against a rectangular window.
keyPoints: Parametric form: P(u) = P1 + u(P2-P1), u∈[0,1]
Four inequalities from boundaries: xwmin≤x≤xwmax, ywmin≤y≤ywmax
Rewrite as: p_i u ≤ q_i where p_i are direction components
p1=-Δx, p2=Δx, p3=-Δy, p4=Δy
q1=x1-xwmin, q2=xwmax-x1, q3=y1-ywmin, q4=ywmax-y1
For parallel edges (p_i=0), check if q_i<0 for rejection
For non-parallel: calculate r_i = q_i/p_i
u1 = max(0, all r_i where p_i<0)
u2 = min(1, all r_i where p_i>0)
If u1>u2, line is outside; else clip from u1 to u2

questions.question: Explain the Sutherland-Hodgman polygon clipping algorithm. Illustrate with a diagram showing how a concave polygon is clipped against one boundary.
keyPoints: Algorithm clips polygon against each boundary sequentially
Process each edge of polygon: test start and end vertices
Four cases for processing vertices:
Case 1: Both inside - add end vertex to output
Case 2: First inside, second outside - add intersection only
Case 3: First outside, second inside - add intersection and end vertex
Case 4: Both outside - add nothing
After processing all edges against one boundary, output becomes input for next boundary
Continue for all four boundaries
Handles convex polygons efficiently
May produce extra vertices at intersections
Limitations with concave polygons

questions.question: Write the algorithm for Cohen-Sutherland line clipping. Also write pseudocode for Liang-Barsky algorithm and compare their time complexities.
keyPoints: Cohen-Sutherland: Assign outcodes to endpoints, test trivial cases, compute intersections if needed, iterate until fully processed
Pseudocode: Calculate Δx, Δy; initialize u1=0, u2=1; for each boundary compute p and q values; update u1, u2; if u1>u2 reject else compute clipped endpoints
Time complexity: Cohen-Sutherland O(1) trivial, O(k) where k is number of subdivisions; Liang-Barsky O(1) with at most 4 computations
Liang-Barsky generally more efficient for non-trivial cases
Both are output-sensitive to number of clipped edges
Space: both O(1)
